---
title: "Client Report - Flights: Column Aggregation"
subtitle: "Unit 2 Task 2"
author: "Mitzi Vite"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl
import requests #new for reading in JSON
import io       #new for reading in JSON
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```



```{python}
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# Include and execute your code here
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/byuidatascience/data4missing/master/data-raw/flights_missing/flights_missing.json"
response = requests.get(url)
df = pl.read_json(io.BytesIO(response.content))
```



## QUESTION 1

__Calculate the sample size (i.e. number of rows in the dataset) for each airport in each year__. What do you notice?

_The dataset consists of 7 airports registered over various years. Normally, each year should consist of 12 rows for each airport, one row for each month of that year. However, there are fewer rows than expected in some years, meaning some missing data. Also, there are some rows without the name of the airport._

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
print(df.columns)
result = df.group_by(["airport_name", "year"]).agg(
  pl.len().alias("sample_size")
).sort(["airport_name", "year"])
result
# print(result["airport_name"].n_unique())
# print(result["airport_name"].unique().sort())
```

## QUESTION 2

__Which airport has the worst delays?__ Describe the metric you chose, and why you chose it to determine the “worst” airport. Your answer should include a summary table that lists (for each airport) the total number of flights, total number of delayed flights, proportion of delayed flights, and average delay time in hours._

_The metric I chose was the proportion of delayed flights, since it allows fair comparison regardless of airport size. San Francisco International has the highest proportion at 26.1% and the longest average delay at 0.27 hours per flight, making it the worst airport in the dataset. Salt Lake City performs best with only 14.7% of flights delayed. Some rows have no airport name recorded, which represents missing data that could affect these results._

```{python}
summary = df.group_by("airport_name").agg([
    pl.col("num_of_flights_total").sum().alias("total_flights"),
    pl.col("num_of_delays_total").sum().alias("total_delayed"),
    (pl.col("num_of_delays_total").sum() / pl.col("num_of_flights_total").sum()).alias("proportion_delayed"),
    (pl.col("minutes_delayed_total").sum() / pl.col("num_of_flights_total").sum() / 60).alias("average_delay_hours")
])
summary

```

## QUESTION 3

__Create a barplot showing the proportion of all flights that are delayed at each airport. Describe what you learn from this graph.__  

_The barplot shows the proportion of delayed flights for each airport. San Francisco International has the highest proportion of delayed flights, while Salt Lake City has the lowest. This visualization confirms the summary statistics and highlights the variability in delay performance across different airports._

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
plot_data = {
  "airport_name": summary["airport_name"].to_list(),
  "proportion_delayed": summary["proportion_delayed"].to_list(),
}

(
  ggplot(plot_data, aes(x="airport_name", y="proportion_delayed")) +
    geom_bar(stat="identity") +
    coord_flip() +
    labs(
        title="Proportion of Delayed Flights by Airport",
        x="Airport",
        y="Proportion Delayed"
    )
)

```
